metadata.notes:
agent_supports: unknown
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: unknown
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a

Fresh-column reassessment. STOP at Stage 1 (applicable:false) — no recipe, no recording.

## What this scenario requires

background-subagent's defining shape (scenario-meanings.md) is a subagent dispatched to run DETACHED in the BACKGROUND (fire-and-forget) while the parent turn continues: the parent finishes its own reply but the session stays `working` as long as the live background child is alive, `SubagentCount(parent)` INCLUDES that live child, and the parent transitions `→ ready` only after the last background child drains. The distinguishing primitive is an ASYNCHRONOUS / fire-and-forget dispatch whose call returns immediately while a child keeps running concurrently (claudecode's `run_in_background` / `SendMessage({to})`).

## agent_supports: unknown

OpenCode DOES ship a first-class subagent primitive — the `task` tool — and the `opencode/foreground-subagent` cell is recorded `yes/full/ready` against it. But those are FOREGROUND task-tool children the parent AWAITS: the parent's turn stays open across each child's lifetime and the parent's `step-finish reason=stop` only fires after every `task` part has flipped to `completed` (see the foreground-subagent assessment). That is the foreground arc — the parent does NOT finish its own reply early and continue while a detached child runs.

I found no documented fire-and-forget BACKGROUND / async subagent dispatch in OpenCode: no `run_in_background`-style task dispatch that returns immediately while the child runs detached, and no SendMessage-to-background-child / async-teammate primitive analogous to claudecode 2.1.77+. OpenCode's `task` tool is synchronous-await by construction. Whether OpenCode exposes (or will expose) a genuinely detached background-subagent dispatch is undetermined from the available docs/binary surface — hence `unknown` rather than `no`. The foreground case is already covered by opencode/foreground-subagent.

## Why the route is STOP even if a primitive existed

Even granting a hypothetical background-dispatch primitive, the cell would not be recordable with the current tooling: drive-opencode-interactive.sh drives BLOCKING headless `opencode run` subprocesses (handles only send|wait_turn|sleep) and steps run strictly SEQUENTIALLY — each `send` shells out to `opencode run` and waits for the turn to fully complete before the next step. There is no async/queue/observe-mid-turn step, so the driver cannot drive or observe a concurrently-running background child WHILE the parent turn is still in flight. A headless recording would only ever capture back-to-back COMPLETED turns, never the parent-held-while-child-alive window the spec asserts.

Per the #476 routing in implement/SKILL.md precondition 2, `agent_supports ∈ {unknown}` → STOP → applicable:false. by_adapter.opencode is set applicable:false with a scope_note; no recipe, no recording. (The driver gap is noted as a secondary blocker but the primary frozen reason is agent_supports=unknown.)
caveats: OpenCode's `task` tool IS a real subagent primitive (foreground-subagent is recorded yes/full/ready); the unknown verdict is specific to the BACKGROUND/detached/async variant this scenario requires, not to subagents generally.

If a future OpenCode release ships a fire-and-forget background-dispatch primitive (run_in_background-style task), reassess agent_supports — observability would likely be `full` (opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter and the daemon reads parent/child state from SQLite, the same store-read path foreground-subagent proves), but the headless driver would still need a live-REPL / async-observe primitive to record it.

background-subagent (detached agent session with a session_id) is distinct from background-process (a shell process via run_in_background bash) — opencode/background-process is separately assessed no/incapable.

sources.note: Defining shape: subagent runs DETACHED in the background while the parent continues; parent stays working until the last background child drains; SubagentCount(parent) includes the live child. Distinguishing primitive = fire-and-forget / async dispatch.

opencode's `task` tool spawns FOREGROUND children the parent AWAITS (yes/full/ready) — the parent's step-finish reason=stop fires only after every task part completes. This is the foreground arc, NOT the detached/background dispatch this scenario requires.

Driver handles only send|wait_turn|sleep; each `send` shells out to BLOCKING `opencode run` and steps run sequentially — no async/queue step to drive or observe a concurrently-running background child mid-turn.
features.subagents: true covers the (foreground) task-tool primitive; there is no separate background-dispatch feature key, and no evidence of a detached/async subagent dispatch in opencode.

Reference for the distinguishing primitive: claudecode 2.1.77+ run_in_background / SendMessage({to}) is a fire-and-forget background dispatch (yes/full/ready). No opencode analogue found — hence opencode agent_supports=unknown.

recipe.notes: scope_note: opencode subagents are FOREGROUND task-tool children the parent AWAITS — opencode ships the `task` tool (opencode/foreground-subagent is recorded yes/full/ready) but no documented fire-and-forget BACKGROUND / async subagent dispatch (no run_in_background-style task that returns immediately while a detached child runs, no SendMessage-to-background-child primitive). agent_supports=unknown → STOP at Stage 1 (applicable:false), no recipe, no recording; the foreground case is already covered by opencode/foreground-subagent. Secondary blocker: even granting a hypothetical background primitive, the headless driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh, send|wait_turn|sleep over BLOCKING sequential `opencode run`) cannot drive or observe a concurrently-running background child mid-turn. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/background-subagent/assessment.json (unknown / n/a / n/a).
